metadata.title: Nilanjana | Design Portfolio
metadata.description: Portfolio of Nilanjana — a design student specializing in UI/UX, branding, and visual communication.

profile.name: Nilanjana
profile.bio:
I'm a design student passionate about creating meaningful visual experiences that sit at the intersection of aesthetics and function. My work spans UI/UX design, brand identity, and visual communication — driven by a belief that great design should feel effortless.

I'm currently pursuing my degree in Communication Design, where I've developed a strong foundation in design thinking, typography, and human-centered design. I'm always exploring new ways to push creative boundaries while keeping the user at the center of every decision.

projects.title: Habitica App Redesign
projects.shortDescription: A UX redesign of Habitica — an RPG-style habit tracker — addressing onboarding friction, interaction overload, and punishing game mechanics.
projects.description:
Habitica turns habit-building into a role-playing game, but its dense mechanics create significant barriers for new users. This project began with a structured gap analysis across four dimensions: confusing onboarding that assumed RPG familiarity, interaction friction from unintuitive task completion flows, a broken reward loop where pixelated art and Gold spending felt meaningless, and shallow long-term progression that led to drop-off.

The redesign strategy was grounded in James Clear's habit framework — make it Obvious, Attractive, Easy, and Satisfying. The existing information architecture was mapped in full before a leaner redesigned version was proposed, removing redundant nodes and clarifying the separation between Habits, Dailies, and To-Dos.

New wireframes were produced for core screens — Dailies, Habits, Trophy Room, Character Creation — followed by a redesigned user flow from login through tutorial to the gameplay loop. A 5-step tutorial screen guides new users progressively. The revised colour scheme replaces high-saturation purple with Lavender Grey (calm, focus) and Dark Ash (contrast), and new screens for Leaderboard, Terrain Choice, Side Menu, and Campfire round out the redesigned experience.

images.alt: Habitica app overview — Habits, Dailies, To-Dos explained
images.caption: Understanding the app: Habits, Dailies, To-Dos, and the core RPG mechanics

images.caption: Gap 3: Pixelated rewards and broken Gold motivation loop weaken the feedback cycle

images.alt: Gap — progression and long-term retention
images.caption: Gap 4: Repetitive customisation and shallow RPG depth cause long-term drop-off

projects.title: Delhi Tourism Pamphlet
projects.shortDescription: A print pamphlet for Delhi Tourism spotlighting Qutub Minar, blending traditional Indian visual motifs with clear, visitor-friendly information design.
projects.description:
This project was a print design brief to create a tourism pamphlet promoting Delhi — with Qutub Minar as its hero landmark. The challenge was balancing rich cultural storytelling with practical visitor information across a multi-panel foldout that people would actually read on location.

The visual language draws on traditional Indian decorative motifs — lotus patterns, geometric borders, and architectural line illustration — rendered in a warm saffron and deep crimson palette echoing Mughal-era craftsmanship. The cover pairs bold typography with the iconic monument illustration to command attention on a display rack.

The interior spreads cover everything a visitor needs: monument facts and history, the evening light show schedule, transport options from Indira Gandhi International Airport and Delhi Metro, accommodation categories, a full Delhi itinerary spanning Chandni Chowk, Red Fort, India Gate, Lotus Temple, Qutub Minar, Humayun's Tomb, Lodhi Garden, and Jantar Mantar, plus curated shopping destinations (Dilli Haat, Janpath Market) and dining areas. Contact details and a QR code link to the Delhi Tourism app.

images.alt: Feature spread — Qutub Minar with contact and booking details
images.caption: Hero spread: bold typographic prompt with monument photography and booking CTA

images.alt: About the Monument, Schedule, and Also Visit
images.caption: Monument history, light show timings, and nearby attractions in a two-column grid

images.alt: Things to Know, How to Get Around, Where to Stay, Welcome to Delhi
images.caption: Practical visitor information: language, transport options, accommodation guide

images.alt: Full Delhi itinerary, Where to Shop, Where to Eat
images.caption: Complete Delhi guide: landmark itinerary, shopping destinations, and dining areas

projects.title: Neeman's — Domain Study
projects.shortDescription: A brand and market study of Neeman's, India's first Merino Wool footwear brand, examining its D2C strategy, digital gaps, and rapid offline expansion.
projects.description:
Neeman's is a Hyderabad-based D2C footwear brand founded in 2018 around a single idea: one shoe for every occasion, made from Merino Wool and recycled PET. This domain study examined how a sustainability-first brand built a loyal customer base — and where the gaps lie.

The research mapped the brand's digital core (1.42M monthly visits, 39.6% aged 25–34, GoKwik-powered conversion optimisation, 4.4K organic keywords) against two identified gaps: a men-centric product range in a female-dominated market (women hold 54.36% of Indian footwear market share), and social media traffic disproportionately driven by Facebook despite an Instagram-native audience. Strategy recommendations addressed both: women-specific silhouettes with premium textures, a WFORWOMEN collaboration concept, and a lifestyle content pivot centred on "Day in Life with Neeman's".

The study also analysed the viral micro-influencer campaign "Are Those Neeman's Shoes?" — a guerrilla phone-algorithm hack that drove organic traffic spikes during IPL — and proposed a poster concept linking the brand's recycled PET bottles to the Genie-in-a-lamp story. The section closes with a new logo design proposal for retail and Instagram contexts.

images.alt: Brand overview — Neeman's origin and philosophy
images.caption: Origin story: a travel packing problem solved with Merino Wool and a family name

projects.title: Kantha — Craft Research
projects.shortDescription: A research study on Kantha embroidery from Birbhum, West Bengal, proposing three design interventions to sustain the craft and empower its artisan community.
projects.description:
Kantha is a centuries-old embroidery tradition from the Birbhum district of West Bengal, practised almost exclusively by women from Scheduled Caste communities in the Bolpur cluster. This research project examined the craft end-to-end — from the laterite landscape of Birbhum to the motifs (Maachh, Padma, Mayur, Mandir), the five-step production process (Design, Printing, Embroidery, Stitching, Finishing), the cluster structure, and the full value chain from raw material procurement through to boutique and export retail.

Socio-cultural research revealed that market pressures have pushed designs toward commercial aesthetics, eroding traditional patterns. The stakeholder map spans master artisans, local micro-entrepreneurs, wholesalers, boutique owners, and NIFT researchers — each playing a distinct role in the craft's survival.

Three interventions were proposed. The Artisan Saarthi Portal is a Bengali-language mobile platform connecting artisans to Aadhaar-linked Artisan Cards, government welfare schemes, and a co-operative finder — with billboard and digital promotion planned for the Bolpur cluster. Co-operative Development addresses collective bargaining, with phased skill modules in marketing, photography, and entrepreneurship. The third intervention, a NIFT Craft Archive, creates an online research database documenting cluster evolution across campuses, giving future designers a living record to build upon.

images.alt: Birbhum region and West Bengal context
images.caption: Locating Kantha: Birbhum district, Bolpur cluster, and the Kopal River valley

images.alt: Socio-cultural factors shaping Kantha craft
images.caption: Predominantly female SC community artisans managing craft and household responsibilities
